sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. James, a 63-year-old male, presents with a 5-month history of constant pain in his left shoulder and elbow, which started after a fall 5 months ago while walking up stairs.
He reports the pain is present all the time and can be quite severe.
He has been trying to maintain his exercise routine, but the pain worsens with lifting, particularly with weights, and has limited him to lifting only 15-20 pounds.
He has tried icing and has stopped lifting weights, but the pain persists.
Past medical history: Not mentioned.
Review of systems: No mention of other symptoms in other organs.
Current medications: Not mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The left shoulder and elbow were tender to palpation, with no warmth or erythema.
The Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer's tests were positive.
Proximal and distal upper extremity strength was normal, with intact median, radial, and ulnar sensation.
Abduction to 90 degrees was normal, and the empty can test was normal.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I believe Mr. James is experiencing impingement syndrome of his left shoulder, with secondary pain in the wrist due to the connection between the two joints.
Problem list: Impingement syndrome of left shoulder; wrist pain secondary to shoulder impingement; unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed no medications, but advised Mr. James to continue icing, especially his shoulder, and rest as much as possible.
I requested a referral for Dr. Martha Rivera to be seen in physical therapy two days per week.
I will follow up with Mr. James to assess his progress and determine if further treatment is necessary.
Follow-up: I will follow up with Mr. James in the future to assess his progress and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
